speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, Lidia Eichenholz. This is tape four, yes. And this is in Minneapolis, Minnesota, and the date today is November 2, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's perhaps talk a little bit about how you gained false identity. Did you have a new name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had-- the man who took me out. I mean, he and his-- they provided me with a new name, which was, I think I mentioned in the papers, Helena Kaminska, Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helena, Helena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena Kaminska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaminska.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and-- but the papers were forged, but they are-- are not well made. So the main thing to us to avoid showing them to anybody because it would be easy to verify.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they were false?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then, of course, there was this fear that-- that I might be recognized and it will involve-- by some of-- you know, by some of the people that I knew before. Although I have not been to school there. I went to school in Rowne. So I really did not know that many kids in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met some, but-- but-- I-- I had it in one of my stories that you have but not-- not much happened. They just let it go. In the meantime, there was a turning point in the war because Stalingrad fell in spring of '43. And all the collaborators realized that they have to leave, especially the Eastern Poland, that the wall-- although at least it stood for a while. But it-- but-- the victory was escaping German. So many people from-- from Rowne, Dubno, were going to Lwow and then trying to get west, far west as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were those who-- who-- who-- I don't-- who were afraid of Russians coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the-- when the-- when the-- when Stalingrad--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fell the whole outlook changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it looked like--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that-- the whole tide of war-- there were many prisoners taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: German prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: German prisoners and the rest was in bad shape. They had to be transported to Lwow hospitals, military hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: They usually did it at night, never the last number spread. And the German commanding officer, general, under Stalingrad was taken prisoner. Was it [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]? I think-- anyway, that could not be hidden. So Lwow became flooded with refugees from the east. And the German army--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they actually-- the war-- you know, the war still lasted. That was only '43. So the German army, actually, they [? dropped ?] and fought the war. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was just that one place in Stalingrad where they had failed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they are failing on all others, but not that dramatically. The siege of Stalingrad lasted a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back a little bit to your life in-- in Lwow with false identity papers. And was the name chosen by you, or were you given the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I-- I-- really, they just gave me the name. I really did not care what it was. It just seemed to be easy to remember. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone stop you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a couple of minor accidents, but nothing really serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you meet during these minor accidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as I said before, I met some friends from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the Germans stop you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no Germans-- I-- I actually had pretty good luck. Even if they stopped me, they believed my papers and let it go and...

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was it that stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city sometimes, they were trying to get people to work in Germany. So they would stop and just ask for the papers. But since I already worked for the Germans, they would let it go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't know they were false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they do not cop to it, but the city was becoming very flooded with refugees, and I was afraid it will happen again. So they were always recruiting people for the work in Germany. So-- so I volunteer for--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the next step-- you volunteered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I volunteered for-- they say-- there was-- they-- they actually wanted non-Jewish young people to go and work too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went as a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a non-Jew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you suspected of being a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite, not-- if I was, I did not-- nothing happened. I did not know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lwow to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Langenbielau, to Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Langenbielau, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Silesia, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Silesia. And when you got there, what kind of housing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was at beginning as a working camp. So the housing, again, many people to one room. It was not so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, it was just a working camp. So you had little more freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could leave if you wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- not quite, but-- but-- but you could leave in a group, let's say, not alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then come back later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And be back, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you paid money, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, but we had some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did give you food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some food, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some housing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Clothing and-- Well, just, you know, type of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some people had better jobs than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, again, I was cleaning and I-- I did not have any-- any special training. But I did learn enough German in the meantime so I could translate. And that help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your age at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- I must have been 17 or 18. 18 was.

speaker: interviewer
text: 18 years of age. How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there-- according to my papers, I am there until the war ended, but actually, Russians got there earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Russians get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they get there in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: In April of--

speaker: interviewer
text: '45, And what was it like when they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we expected them but we did not quite know. And then the tanks came and the people came to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- to-- to greet them. It-- actually, in-- in Schindler's paper in the movie, the description, or in the book of Thomas Keneally, the description is much-- is more or less accurate. We obviously asked them for food, but they did not have the food themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have enough themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to-- so we had to go and get some food from neighboring places. Sometimes, they would give it. Anyway, I decided to go right away back to Rowne to see what happened to rest of my family. I did not have information about my mother, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about your father, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: About my father-- but I still-- I still had a lot of relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know what had happened to your mother or your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so the Russians came--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In April, 1945. And at that point, you were free to do what you wished.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you-- yes, yes. You had to get a paper to go back east.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my home. Although this part of Poland was becoming-- obviously, would be Russian, wouldn't be anymore Poland. And-- and I-- I went first to Lwow and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to travel with those army trucks. The train, the rails, were still damaged. I couldn't-- it would have been a much better traveling arrangement with the train, but the rails were still damaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to your home, and was your home still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but that was in Dubno. One-- somebody else lived there, and they would not let me in. They didn't know anything. So I had to-- as I went again, I went back to Dubno. There was a maid who lived in Dubno, and she saved some of those pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she your maid when you were very young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. She was actually what they used to call at this time nanny or [? nania. ?] She was since I was born. She's very devoted to our family. So she tried to save pictures of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: She just did that on her own.

speaker: interviewee
text: On her own, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, after you saw her, was it a pleasant greeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, beginning, I was thinking maybe my-- I heard the rumor that maybe my brother is with her but that all proved to be false. So-- but she let me eventually stay with her for a couple of days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hear the rumor about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people-- that was still during the war when I met some of-- after the war, when I met some people in Lwow that I knew. They just made up the story. And then [INAUDIBLE] it was far from the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then after you stayed with the maid, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then it became clear that this part of Poland is going to be rationed. Actually, they announced it and they say all the people who had Polish citizenship before, with the exception of Ukraine-- there was exchange of population-- have to go to the west. And obviously, Stalin did not want it to have many-- [PAUSES FOR  SECONDS] many Poles, or for that matter, even those few Jews who survive in this part of the country. He wanted top-- to be strictly Ukraine, which is-- which is now. And they would provide the freight trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The freight trains. I mean, you know-- cattle trains. Actually, some of them are passengers' trains. but they had so many barges that nobody wanted to. It was already in spring. You'd much rather get wet than eaten by all those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many things with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you could actually tell all that you can carry. Some people took more things because they had many in the family and they could. I didn't-

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] But you had lost everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I lost everything anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went on a train. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we first thought that all the trains were going to Warsaw. But then when we came to Warsaw-- and it took a while on those trains when they-- we stopped in Lublin and all that. And then we heard about uprising in Warsaw. There was a big uprising. Most of the city was destroyed, all of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uprising of--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Pole-- Russians were led on one side of the Vistula and Polish government in London thought that if they could catch the-- if they could get-- make uprising and get hold-- hold of the city, chase the Germans out, then the Russians will come to their-- to their help. Maybe they can declare [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] themself as a government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the war? This is--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, that was the war was still going on-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was still the war was going on in the last-- that started in January, and the war ended in May--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before it ended, OK. So when you went-- you went to Warsaw from--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Warsaw from-- actually, I went back to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Lwow and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but when you got to Warsaw--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard it even before the uprising fell. And I think almost a million people died because eventually German got the upper hand. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they exiled all the population to the concentration camps and many older people just did not survive. And some-- some just died in--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was after-- after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- no, no, last few months of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when that happened, yes. But when you went-- when did you go to Warsaw, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went after the war, but the uprising took place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was just before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to Warsaw, there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing. The whole city was destroyed. Matter of fact, it was a rainy day, and as we were approaching, the train was going slow to Warsaw. People were jumping on our train. We were crowded anyway, and we asked them why they are doing such a stupid thing. And they say, yes, well, they have to. It-- it was raining-- that they have to escape the rain. So it's better to be on a crowded trains. They apparently did not worry that much about bed bugs as-- as we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed very shortly because there was no place to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- then people say, Lodz is on the other side of Vistula. So somebody said that in Lodz, and I knew some people-- a dentist. I might find place to stay. And I went there but that worked for a very short time. I stayed with a dentist. And she let me sleep in her dental chair. But it-- but she had so many patients, they would-- you know, I had to wait late in the evening and get up very early to let her have this chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: To have your chair to sleep on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't very comfortable either. So I met some friends. And we decided to go to Breslau because apparently, it was not so crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau-- it's a bit Polish-- German city which became Polish after the war. Still is an old university town, very famous. And over there, they said that the housing would not be such a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, over there, we-- when we came to it, it was really almost a walled city, just Russian soldiers. Many-- of population fled. So I tried to get a job. But there were very few grocery stores, just there were for the bread. And by then, well, we heard that-- and we decided that the best thing was to leave Poland altogether because if you don't do it now, you may never do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was this? How long were you in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breslau, I was about all through the summer '45. And at this time, many people from Israel, from agency were arriving and saying that there is a legal wait. Though Israel was mandated there's a legal wait to go to Israel. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- and they took transports of people. And they could not take them openly because Israel was actually closed for-- for new immigrants. So they were taking them through various southern places and sometimes through Italy and sometimes through France. And then they were boarding-- there was a blockade. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were boarding in those ships were willing to-- to break the blockade for money. Most of-- it did not always succeeded. They were frequently interned in Cyprus and had to wait. But we did not go all the way. You didn't go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when you joined these organizations-- this one, I know you went with them just for a short-- for a short while. Then also the Germans who still remain in Poland were leaving Germany-- were leaving Poland for Germany. So we went with them until-- we have to go first to East Germany. And then-- then we crossed to West Germany. I mean, we went first to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not stay in Berlin but this was so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unsecure, and somebody said that Munich would be the best place because of American occupation. But we could not go. You could not just choose where you go. So we had to go first to British occupations on-- in Hanover. Actually, this thing I must say, the trains were crowded like-- like a beehive. You just had to climb and hang, and they could never collect any money or anything. And we had very little by way of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] luggage, only backpacks. And somehow, we got to Munich. We still had qualms. And then we registered. They said that the university will open and we registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they had such a-- for refugee, UNRRA was in Munich, UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had such various places. It was all very primitive, but they give you some soup and-- and we stayed there and registered for school. And then there was still a little time left. So we-- we went to-- we decided to explore Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- and we did. We didn't know what's going on, but we got as far as Vienna, but Vienna was full of Russians. So Vienna was a divided city [INAUDIBLE] later with [INAUDIBLE]. So-- So we-- so we came back to Germany and started at school. I was with a group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five years, and you finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started in '45, and I finished in '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: '50 with a degree?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had the degree of medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doctor of medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did my doctorate even with my trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then-- then displaced person bill came. And I-- and I came to-- to United States on the-- you know, they provided boats. Everything was free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And at first-- at first, I came to Pomona, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was engaged to my former husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed in Pomona for a couple times working in the hospitals and applying for internships. And then he came from-- from Munich, and he stayed in Washington DC. And I could not get internship for him in-- in California, so I joined him at the hospital [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the nation's capital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married there and the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and then where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did my internship. So they provided-- they paid very little money, $25 a month or $50 sometimes, but they provided room and board.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened after you finished your intern?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to do residency. I did it in Jersey City Medical Center.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the pay was a little better. And then-- then he was-- that was during the [INAUDIBLE] war. So he was drafted into the army. I still stayed behind. And then I joined him later. He got a job with [INAUDIBLE] Army Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Denver. And I joined him there. And-- and then I worked at [INAUDIBLE] General, which is a University Hospital in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was born later when we moved to Minneapolis in '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: In '56-- and at the present time, you're retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the present time, I'm-- I am divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Divorced and retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And retired and I have lots of problems with my health. I-- I-- I had a couple other pregnancies, but-- actually, one very bad one and-- because of high blood pressure. And right now, my blood pressure is-- is too low. I had-- I had problem with my heart. I had angioplasty. And what's more important, I had problem with my leg. I have a pinched nerve, and I used to walk a lot, and now I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell us some kind of thought you have regarding all this that has happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wish my present would be better than it is. But just-- the past is past and I always-- in my-- in my stories, I always try-- they're never sentimental. I try to make them more adventurous. And unfortunately, because of court proceedings with my husband, I am in a precarious position economically.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what could you leave with people who are going to be watching this as-- as a final thought about this-- this horrible time which we call the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course it was horrible, but as I said before, the Holocaust victims-- those-- I mean, those who died, of course, we know. But I feel myself kind of also stigmatized although I did not believe it before because I was doing well. But now I cannot cope with major stresses or any [INAUDIBLE]. And lack of security When you get older and handicapped in some way-- that's my personal opinion. Of course, everybody else was talking about other things. So I won't repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how about the pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- we will get to the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we have speed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture is of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my father during the World War I. He was going to school in Belgium in Liege. And he volunteered when Belgium was-- the whole class actually volunteered. And here he's in uniform. It was a terrible slaughter, but he survived it quite well. And after the war, he came back to Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'll- I'll-- I'll ask you-- Now, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. My mother provided me with this outfit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- where-- when and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Lwow. It was the very first time I tried to leave ghetto and live on the other side. But then I was too endangered and I had to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street, some photographer. They take-- they-- they take it and they sell you it immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned about their taking the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not particularly-- I was too young at this time to be-- and I looked older in this outfit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it-- ot wasn't any danger to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, true, everything-- but it was potentially dangerous. Except I could [?cope?] better than I do now. I was done with my medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in 1950 in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it was taken for some documents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there are two pre-war pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, let's-- These pictures were the ones saved by your maid, Irene?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they are of--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father and that's my mother. As you see, just so shortly before the war but they did not seem to be concerned. They were just traveling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You see this--

speaker: interviewer
text: These are-- this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then you see those people. That must have been Saturday. And that's my brother, Joseph. A small beret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, beret, yes. That looks nice on him too. Good looking young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and I have about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Is that where you'd like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling now.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose when maybe when she was pregnant with me or maybe right after.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother-- my mother married young. So she must have been, I don't know, maybe 21 or 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture. It's very beautiful. Thank you so much, Lidia.
